questions.question: What is the size of the 'int' data type in Java?
questions.explanation: In Java, int is always a 32-bit signed integer, which is 4 bytes, regardless of the platform.

questions.question: What is the output of: int x = 5; System.out.println(x++);
questions.explanation: The post-increment operator (x++) returns the current value first, then increments. So it prints 5, and x becomes 6 after.

questions.question: How do you declare an array of 10 integers in Java?
questions.explanation: In Java, arrays are objects and must be created using the 'new' keyword. The correct syntax is: int[] arr = new int[10];

questions.question: What is the result of: 10 % 3 in Java?
questions.explanation: The modulus operator (%) returns the remainder of division. 10 divided by 3 is 3 with a remainder of 1.

questions.question: What is the default value of an int array element in Java?
questions.explanation: When an int array is created, all elements are automatically initialized to 0, the default value for int type.

questions.question: Which loop is guaranteed to execute at least once?
questions.explanation: The do-while loop checks the condition after executing the loop body, so it always executes at least once.

questions.question: What is the output of: System.out.println(5 / 2);
questions.explanation: Since both operands are integers, integer division is performed, which truncates the decimal part. Result is 2, not 2.5.

questions.question: What is the valid range of a byte data type in Java?
questions.explanation: The byte data type is an 8-bit signed integer with a range from -128 to 127.

questions.question: What will be the output? int[] arr = {1, 2, 3}; System.out.println(arr[3]);
questions.explanation: Array indices start from 0. For an array of size 3, valid indices are 0, 1, 2. Accessing index 3 throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

questions.question: Which operator is used for logical AND in Java?
questions.explanation: The && operator is the logical AND operator. The & is bitwise AND. && also provides short-circuit evaluation.

questions.question: What is the output? for(int i=0; i<3; i++) { System.out.print(i + " "); }
questions.explanation: The loop starts at i=0 and continues while i<3. It prints 0, 1, and 2, then stops when i becomes 3.

questions.question: What is the default value of a boolean variable in Java?
questions.explanation: The default value of a boolean instance variable is false. Local variables must be explicitly initialized.

questions.question: What is the output? int x = 10; if(x > 5) System.out.println("A"); else System.out.println("B");
questions.explanation: Since x (10) is greater than 5, the condition is true, so "A" is printed.

questions.question: How do you find the length of an array in Java?
questions.explanation: Arrays in Java have a 'length' property (not a method), so you use arr.length without parentheses.

questions.question: What is the output? int i = 1; while(i <= 3) { System.out.print(i + " "); i++; }
questions.explanation: The while loop starts with i=1 and continues while i<=3, printing 1, 2, 3, then stops when i becomes 4.

questions.question: What is the size of the 'double' data type in Java?
questions.explanation: The double data type is a 64-bit (8 bytes) double-precision floating-point number.

questions.question: What is the output? int x = 5; System.out.println(++x);
questions.explanation: The pre-increment operator (++x) increments the value first, then returns it. So x becomes 6, then prints 6.

questions.question: Which statement is used to exit a loop prematurely in Java?
questions.explanation: The 'break' statement is used to exit a loop immediately. 'continue' skips to the next iteration.

questions.question: What is the result of the expression: true && false?
questions.explanation: The logical AND (&&) operator returns true only if both operands are true. Since one is false, the result is false.

questions.question: What is the output? int[] arr = new int[5]; System.out.println(arr[0]);
questions.explanation: When an int array is created, all elements are automatically initialized to 0, the default value for int.

questions.question: What is the output? int x = 10; if(x % 2 == 0) System.out.println("Even"); else System.out.println("Odd");
questions.explanation: The modulus operator (%) returns the remainder. 10 % 2 equals 0, so the condition is true and "Even" is printed.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly declares a 2D array in Java?
questions.explanation: A 2D array is declared using int[][] arr = new int[rows][columns]; syntax. This creates a 3x4 array.

questions.question: What is the output? for(int i=5; i>0; i--) { if(i==3) break; System.out.print(i + " "); }
questions.explanation: The loop prints 5, then 4. When i becomes 3, the break statement exits the loop before printing 3.

questions.question: What is the result of: 5 > 3 ? 10 : 20 in Java?
questions.explanation: This is the ternary operator (condition ? value_if_true : value_if_false). Since 5>3 is true, it returns 10.

questions.question: What is the output? int i = 0; do { System.out.print(i + " "); i++; } while(i < 3);
questions.explanation: The do-while loop executes the body first, then checks the condition. It prints 0, 1, 2, then stops when i becomes 3.

questions.question: What is the default value of a char variable in Java?
questions.explanation: The default value of a char variable is '\u0000', which is the null character (Unicode 0).

questions.question: What is the output? int[] arr = {10, 20, 30}; for(int x : arr) System.out.print(x + " ");
questions.explanation: This is an enhanced for loop (for-each). It iterates through each element in the array and prints 10, 20, 30.

questions.question: What is the result of: true || false?
questions.explanation: The logical OR (||) operator returns true if at least one operand is true. Since the first operand is true, the result is true.

questions.question: What is the output? for(int i=0; i<5; i++) { if(i==2) continue; System.out.print(i + " "); }
questions.explanation: The continue statement skips the current iteration. When i==2, it skips printing 2 and continues with the next iteration.

questions.question: What is the output? int x = 5, y = 10; int z = (x > y) ? x : y; System.out.println(z);
questions.explanation: The ternary operator checks if x>y. Since 5>10 is false, it returns y (10). So z=10 and prints 10.
